# Transcrição: 06-H-Scale Dupe - Risks Stability in Favour of More Chances of Conversions

## [00:00 - 03:01]

So this is a scaling strategy which up until 2018 was one of my favorite ways of scaling. It was reliable, it was fast and it was just something which enabled you to take a winning campaign, a winning ad set and really scale up faster with some kind of controls in place. So this is a method where you take your winning campaigns and you just simply duplicate it to a higher budget. And you know, back in the day I would duplicate the actual campaign two, three, four times, let the winners stay, kill the losers, etc. And it was fairly reliable. Now in 2018, Facebook made a change which made this less successful. The change was to do with the history of the campaign and ad set level and actually favoring that far more than anything else. So if for example, let's say you're targeting a 3% look alike and you're spending $1,000 a day successfully in that campaign, in the ad set level, etc. Things are going really well, you've stabilized, optimized, everything is great. Back in 2016, 17, 18 for example, I would quite comfortably duplicate that campaign two, three, four times or the ad set and just really scale up. I would go from 1,000 a day to 3,000 to 5,000, etc. With some controls in place, but I generally knew how to make that work. But there's a few things about that. Now back then, the way it used to work was if you had that winning ad set, Facebook seemingly had that knowledge that the ad set works and it would always with your duplications give you great performance as well. The change that was made in 2018, however, was that the auction, the algorithm, things like that started to favor history far more at the ad set level. And what that means is if you imagine taking your ad set that's been performing really well, $1,000 a day, you spent 5, 10K already and things are great. When you go to duplicate that, you're starting with zero data. So what that means is back before when it used to work, although you also had zero data then, there was still knowledge kept on that ad set and the audiences and ads, etc. Which meant that when you duplicated, I believe that knowledge was carried over. Now what Facebook seemed to do, and maybe it was done to stop this, maybe it was done for different reasons, is when you duplicate a winning ad set or in this case for CBO a campaign, it is literally learning again. So this whole thing about learning mode and learning phase, that's always been there. But I believe Facebook have dialed up how much of an impact it has on new ad sets and new campaigns. Now, one thing I know about how Facebook tried to engineer the platform is they don't like you hacking the platform too much.

## [03:01 - 06:04]

And what that means is some of the strategies that used to work with, you know, running 50 ad sets and trimming the losers and pushing the winners and things like that has become so much more unreliable now. It works at small spends, you know, you can maybe keep going at those small levels. But if you're serious about growing your business and profitability and everything off the back of Facebook, they just don't cut it now. So this strategy here is something I don't use that much now. I can't remember the last time I used it. And, you know, there still will be the occasion where I will want to test this out. One thing you should know about how I run my own campaigns is I'm always testing and retesting my strategies. So everything you're seeing here in this training program has been stress tested to the extent that if something has been working, we're always coming back to it. We don't rely on it always working because that's the way Facebook work. That's a core part of the BPM method. The whole brand side of becoming a really good brand marketer that generally does not change. Performance marketer that needs to make this, you know, the whole campaigns work, analysis, etc. That is reliant on the Facebook platform. And the Facebook platform changes every two, three months, even, you know, more frequently as well. And because of that, I'm always reviewing the scaling strategies in particular. So a lot of the testing strategies, optimization, etc. We're always doing it day in, day out. And if there's any change, we know about it and we update our own training, which comes into this program. But the scaling strategies, we end up favoring stability. So this particular strategy of simply copying your winning campaigns with a higher budget isn't our preferred method. The analogy here is going back to the oceans, boats and hooks analogy of looking at how your audience is seen by Facebook and how your ad sets are dropped in the auction and your ads are trying to collect fish, for example. And in this kind of form, no matter what the audience size is, your ad sets and ads are going to be limited by the spend that you've got and the kind of quality metrics in the auction and things like that. But the idea of the H scale Duke strategy is that you take your winning campaigns and you simply duplicate it to the same audiences. So you're able to scale up faster. Now, if you've followed the previous videos, you will know that this introduces risk. So in this given audience here, where, you know, the big circle around these four smaller ones represents your audience, it could be one million, 10 million, 50 million people, whatever it is, what you're doing is duplicating the same winning campaigns into that audience with the expectation that you'll tap into more and more people and you'll be able to control your risks. ## [06:04 - 09:04]

and bump up my budget budgets. I could go nitro and really, really push out faster, or I could do it by duplicating my campaign. So in this scenario, let's say I go from $1,000 a day to 2000s in a week and then 3000, 4000, for example, I'm still scaling up. But for me, I'm doing it in a more safer way. This way I can scale up faster. Like within a within an hour, I can go from spending 1000 to 4000 by duplicating my ad set. So I've got four and four campaigns running. But the problem here is, and if you've been through the training, you'll know this already is audience overlap. And then the other problem is around that whole learning phase that the duplicates you've created have to relearn their own patterns and behaviors again. So if you're duplicating at the higher budget, you're going to get some instability. You definitely need to build that data up. Chances are they won't work immediately right off the back, right off the back in terms of the performance you've seen on the previous ad sets. Not to say they won't, but a either you'd need to start off with smaller budgets and build up, or if you go straight in with bigger budgets, then you're cutting bad performers fast and then moving them on as well. So some of the key things to bear in mind here. Scale versus stability. When you're scaling like this, not only may your new campaigns not perform as well, it can also impact the performance of your existing campaign as well. So this is a strategy which I would not recommend if you're still fairly new to scaling, if Facebook ads is, you know, you're still learning and building confidence up in, for example, because this can create instability in your ad accounts. So you just really, really need to be careful of that. Now, the way I run this is when I'm duplicating my campaigns to a bigger budget, sometimes I pause the original. Now you might look at that and rightly say, why would you pause a campaign that's performing so well? Well, here's the thing. If you introduce those new campaigns and you want to minimize the risk of overlap for your good performer and also preserve the data, then that's one way of doing it. In other cases, I'll let it run, especially if the audience size is big. And I'm talking about like into the many millions, if not tens of millions in the audience, then I might let the original run. Generally, when I'm ready to do this strategy, I might be spending around $500 a day with stability, maybe a thousand on that campaign. Then I duplicate to those bigger budgets, so maybe even thousand, two thousand for the new campaigns. And then I'm watching it really carefully. I want to make sure that those new campaigns are performing well. And if they're not, I'm ready to cut them down in terms of pausing ads and ad sets as well. The other thing about the original ad set is I want to make sure I have

## [09:04 - 12:07]

stability before I even consider using this strategy. So stability for me would be at least kind of 30 to 50 conversions a week. For me, that's at the campaign level, if not the ad set level. But certainly Facebook do suggest that's at the ad set level. And then I want to make sure that I'm getting good, good performance over a period of time. So that kind of five to 10 days period so that I'm getting the stability into my campaigns as well. And then what I'm also doing is I'm not doing this duplication too late in the daytime. So roughly about nine, 10 a.m. I'll make that duplication. Also, my preference is to do it earlier in the week as well, because, you know, buying behaviors can be different across weekdays and weekends. And I want to give myself time to build up performance as well. I would also be running rules as well. And you can see the rules in the rules section for what we'd be running. I wouldn't initially automate everything. I would absolutely have my stop loss rules in place. So stop loss, meaning that if anything goes severely wrong. So for example, you know, let's say your ad to cart cost is $10 roughly on a good campaign. If I spend $20, $30 and I don't get an ad to cart, then I know something's wrong. So those kind of stop loss rules can make sure that even if I duplicate to a higher budget, at least I'm preserving my budget if things go really bad as well. And what I want to do is make sure that I'm keeping a close eye on these campaigns. So hence doing it early in the morning allows me the daytime to keep an eye on performance as well. But it just means that I'm able to make decisions. And then I'm in a position once I understand what's working and what's not to automate. Now, me personally, I might automate far quicker, maybe even from day one. But for you, if you've not done this strategy before, you need to be manually keeping an eye on your performance and making decisions, but then using the rules to cut performance if you need to as well. The other thing I would do about this is I would if I pause an ad or ad set in the daytime because the performance was bad, I would resurrect it the next day at around six to nine AM, for example, and I'd give it a second chance and a third chance to give it the opportunity to perform essentially. And my goal here is when I'm duplicating sideways and all I'm doing is copying what's working and bear in mind this differs from the previous H scale strategy that I'm not duplicating into unique segments. I'm literally just duplicating into exact set ups for the campaigns that's working. What I want to do is making sure I want to make sure I give the CBO enough data. So just like any kind of new CBO campaign launch, I want to make sure I'm giving enough data so that it builds stability as well. So it is a higher risk way of scaling. It's not generally my preferred way, which is V scaling or V scale Nitro,

## [12:08 - 13:18]

but it does have a time and place. It's something which you can also test as well. The scenario I might, for example, test this out on would be if I'm in a limited period sale and, for example, I don't want to do the V scale Nitro because I want to spread my risk in terms of introducing new campaigns and spreading budget across many campaigns. But again, you know, balancing the risk and reward or splitting your campaigns out and potentially introducing auction overlap as well. So, you know, as a recap, it will impact on auction overlap, especially unless your audiences are in the tens of millions. Scale versus stability. It's a big trade off with this as well. Start your ad campaigns early on in the day. Have automated rule set up at least for stop losses. So to stop you losing money and make sure you have enough data in your original campaigns and then to build up for your new campaigns as well so that you can have that stability faster as well. And the bigger, bigger the budgets you start this with, the more chance of reaching stability faster as well.
